questions.question: What is the main function of the pericardium in the heart?
questions.explanation: The pericardium plays crucial roles such as fixing the heart in the mediastinum, protecting against the spread of infection, and limiting acute dilation of the heart, especially the right ventricle.

questions.question: What mediators are released during acute pericarditis?
questions.explanation: The inflammatory process in acute pericarditis induces the release of proinflammatory mediators such as cytokines (interleukin-1, IL-6, TNF-alpha), chemokines, prostaglandins and leukotrienes, which increase vascular permeability.

questions.question: What is the most common viral cause of pericarditis in developed countries?
questions.explanation: In developed countries, the most common etiology is viral, with Coxsackievirus B being the most frequently implicated pathogen, especially type B, followed by other enteroviruses and herpesviruses.

questions.question: Which ECG finding is characteristic of Stage I pericarditis?
questions.explanation: Stage I of acute pericarditis is characterized by diffuse ST-segment elevation with superior concavity in most leads, along with depression of the PR segment (atrial lesion).

questions.question: What type of pericardial fluid is most common in acute pericarditis?
questions.explanation: The most common type of pericardial fluid in acute pericarditis is serofibrinous, characterized by a fibrin exudate that is deposited on the inflamed pericardial surfaces.

questions.question: What maneuver improves pain in acute pericarditis?
questions.explanation: Chest pain from acute pericarditis classically improves by sitting and leaning forward, as this position reduces pressure on the inflamed pericardium and decreases leaf-to-blade contact.

questions.question: What is the most characteristic physical sign of acute pericarditis?
questions.explanation: The pericardial rub is the most characteristic and pathognomonic physical sign of acute pericarditis, produced by the rubbing of the inflamed and fibrin-covered pericardial sheets.

questions.question: Which condition is a noninfectious cause of pericarditis?
questions.explanation: Dressler Syndrome is a non-infectious pericarditis that occurs weeks or months after an acute myocardial infarction, cardiac surgery or trauma, due to an autoimmune reaction to damaged myocardial antigens.

questions.question: How many main criteria are required to diagnose acute pericarditis?
options: At least two out of four
questions.explanation: The diagnosis of acute pericarditis is based on the presence of at least two of the four main criteria: characteristic chest pain, pericardial rub, typical ECG changes, and evidence of pericardial effusion.

questions.question: What medication is recommended as an adjuvant to NSAIDs to prevent recurrences?
questions.explanation: Colchicine is an essential adjuvant in the treatment of acute pericarditis, as it significantly reduces the recurrence rate and accelerates the resolution of symptoms when used together with NSAIDs.

questions.question: What age group does acute pericarditis predominantly affect?
questions.explanation: Acute pericarditis predominantly affects young adults, generally between 20 and 50 years of age, although it can occur at any stage of life.

questions.question: What is the definition of cardiac tamponade?
questions.explanation: Cardiac tamponade occurs when rapid or voluminous accumulation of pericardial fluid elevates intrapericardial pressure, severely restricting ventricular diastolic filling and compromising cardiac output.

questions.question: What imaging modality is the 'gold standard' for pericardial evaluation?
questions.explanation: Cardiac Magnetic Resonance (CMR) is considered the 'gold standard' for detailed evaluation of the pericardium, allowing visualization of effusions, thickening, active inflammation and myocardial involvement (myopericarditis).

questions.question: What is a key differentiator between acute pericarditis and STEMI on ECG?
questions.explanation: A key difference between acute pericarditis and STEMI is the absence of pathological Q waves in pericarditis, whereas STEMI is characterized by them, along with localized, convex ST elevation.

questions.question: Which NSAID is preferred in Dressler Syndrome or coronary heart disease?
questions.explanation: Aspirin (750-1000 mg every 8 hours) is the preferred NSAID option in patients with Dressler syndrome (post-AMI pericarditis) or those with coronary artery disease who are already receiving antiplatelet therapy.

questions.question: What contributes to the characteristic pain in pericarditis?
questions.explanation: The chest pain characteristic of pericarditis is due to inflammation and irritation of the sensory nerve endings in the parietal pericardium, innervated primarily by the phrenic nerve.

questions.question: How is pulsus paradoxus defined?
questions.explanation: Pulsus paradoxus is a key, although not pathognomonic, finding in cardiac tamponade, defined as a drop in systolic blood pressure > 10 mmHg during normal inspiration.

questions.question: How is incessant pericarditis defined?
questions.explanation: Incessant pericarditis is defined as the persistence of symptoms and/or objective evidence of inflammation for more than 4-6 weeks but less than 3 months, without complete remission after discontinuation of treatment.

questions.question: What bacterial pathogen commonly causes purulent pericarditis?
questions.explanation: Purulent bacterial pericarditis, although less common, is serious and can be caused by pathogens such as Staphylococcus aureus, Streptococcus pneumoniae, or Haemophilus influenzae.

questions.question: What biomarker indicates concomitant myocarditis in pericarditis?
questions.explanation: Elevation of ultrasensitive troponins (I or T), present in 30-50% of cases, indicates concomitant myopericarditis, suggesting a greater extension of the inflammatory process and a worse prognosis.

questions.question: What is the recommended duration of colchicine for a first episode?
questions.explanation: For a first acute episode of pericarditis, colchicine is recommended for a minimum of 3 months to reduce the risk of recurrence and accelerate resolution of symptoms.

questions.question: What is the effect of pericardial inflammation on diastolic filling?
questions.explanation: The swollen and thickened pericardium, together with the presence of pericardial fluid, reduces pericardial compliance, restricting diastolic filling of the ventricles and elevating intracardiac pressures.

questions.question: Which symptom is highly suggestive of pericarditis when it radiates?
questions.explanation: The chest pain of acute pericarditis often radiates to the left shoulder, especially the left trapezius area, due to irritation of the phrenic nerve fibers innervating the parietal pericardium.

questions.question: Which finding suggests tamponade on echocardiography?
questions.explanation: On echocardiography, collapse of the right chambers (right atrium in late diastole, right ventricle in early diastole) and respiratory variability of transvalvular flows are key signs of cardiac tamponade.

questions.question: What condition is a common cause of pericarditis in chronic kidney failure?
questions.explanation: Uremia, in patients with chronic renal failure, especially those requiring dialysis, is one of the most frequent causes of pericarditis in this group of patients.

questions.question: What is the approximate recurrence rate after a first episode?
questions.explanation: Acute pericarditis has a tendency to recur, which is seen in approximately 15-30% of patients after a first episode, and can reach 50% after multiple episodes.

questions.question: What position can a patient with severe pericarditis adopt?
questions.explanation: If the pain is severe, the patient can adopt an antalgic position, sitting and leaning forward, as this position usually relieves pericardial pain by reducing pressure.

questions.question: What differentiates Stage III pericarditis from an acute myocardial infarction?
options: T inversion without prior ST elevation
questions.explanation: Stage III of acute pericarditis is characterized by diffuse inversion of the T wave, but without a previous elevation of the ST segment, unlike the evolution of an acute myocardial infarction.

questions.question: When are corticosteroids considered for acute pericarditis?
options: If NSAIDs and colchicine are contraindicated
questions.explanation: Corticosteroids are considered a second-line option, only if NSAIDs and colchicine are contraindicated, not tolerated or ineffective, or in specific etiologies such as autoimmune diseases.

questions.question: What complication involves chronic restriction of diastolic filling?
questions.explanation: Constrictive pericarditis is a long-term complication where persistent inflammation and fibrosis can lead to rigid thickening and occasional calcification of the pericardium, chronically limiting diastolic filling.

questions.question: What autoimmune disease can cause acute pericarditis?
questions.explanation: Autoimmune and inflammatory diseases such as systemic lupus erythematosus (SLE), rheumatoid arthritis, and systemic sclerosis are known causes of noninfectious pericarditis.

questions.question: What type of pericardial fluid mixes with blood in the effusion?
questions.explanation: A hemorrhagic pericardial effusion is characterized by fluid mixed with blood, and may be indicative of neoplasia, trauma, tuberculosis, or associated with anticoagulation.

questions.question: Which marker is excellent for monitoring response to treatment?
questions.explanation: C-Reactive Protein (CRP) is an excellent marker of inflammation in acute pericarditis and is very useful to monitor the response to treatment and guide its duration.

questions.question: What is the minimum restriction period for athletes with myopericarditis?
questions.explanation: For athletes, if myocardial involvement (myopericarditis) is present, the period of physical activity restriction may be 6 months or longer, with reevaluation before resuming exercise.

questions.question: What is a major risk criterion for poor prognosis in pericarditis?
questions.explanation: Fever > 38°C is considered a major criterion of poor prognosis in acute pericarditis, suggesting a more severe or systemic infectious etiology, which requires hospitalization.

questions.question: What symptom is characteristic of acute aortic dissection?
options: Sudden, tearing pain in the back
questions.explanation: Acute aortic dissection presents sudden, tearing pain of maximum intensity from the beginning, often radiating to the back or abdomen, which clearly differentiates it from pericardial pain.

questions.question: What causes restriction of diastolic filling in pericarditis?
questions.explanation: The restriction of ventricular diastolic filling in pericarditis is mainly due to the inflamed and thickened pericardium, together with the presence of pericardial fluid, which reduces its compliance.

questions.question: What infection causes pericarditis in endemic areas?
questions.explanation: In developing countries or endemic areas, tuberculous pericarditis is a significantly more common cause, especially in regions with a high prevalence of tuberculosis.

questions.question: Where is the pericardial rub best heard?
options: Left sternal border low, inclined forward
questions.explanation: The pericardial rub is best heard with the diaphragm of the stethoscope, at the low left sternal border or apex, with the patient seated and leaning forward, and at the end of forced expiration.

questions.question: Which ECG finding is highly specific for pericarditis?
questions.explanation: Depression of the PR segment is a very specific sign of atrial lesion on the ECG of acute pericarditis, supporting the diagnosis along with diffuse ST elevation.

questions.question: What medication should accompany high-dose NSAIDs for gastric protection?
questions.explanation: Concomitant use of a proton pump inhibitor (PPI) such as omeprazole (20-40 mg/day) is recommended for gastric protection during treatment with high-dose NSAIDs.

questions.question: In developed countries, what is the most common identified etiology?
questions.explanation: In developed countries, the most common etiology of acute pericarditis is viral, representing approximately 80-90% of cases with an identifiable cause, often presumed viral in idiopathic cases.

questions.question: What can fibrinous exudate in the pericardium lead to?
questions.explanation: The accumulation of fibrin on the pericardial surfaces due to inflammatory exudation is the cause of the characteristic pericardial rub audible on auscultation, due to rubbing of the inflamed leaves.

questions.question: Why may dyspnea occur in acute pericarditis?
questions.explanation: Dyspnea is common in pericarditis, either due to pain that restricts deep breathing or, in more severe cases, due to the presence of a significant pericardial effusion that restricts cardiac filling.

questions.question: What is the 'Ewart Sign' associated with?
questions.explanation: Ewart's Sign, characterized by dullness on percussion, egophony, and decreased breath sounds at the base of the left lung, is associated with a very large pericardial effusion (>500 mL).

questions.question: How is a moderate pericardial effusion defined by echocardiography?
questions.explanation: In the classification of pericardial effusion by echocardiography, a moderate effusion is defined by a separation between the pericardial leaves of 10-20 mm in diastole.

questions.question: When is pericardiocentesis indicated for etiological studies?
questions.explanation: Analysis of pericardial fluid by pericardiocentesis is indicated if there is suspicion of tamponade, persistent large effusion, or specific etiology (eg, bacterial, tuberculous, neoplastic).

questions.question: What is a common dose of Ibuprofen in acute pericarditis?
questions.explanation: A common dose of Ibuprofen for the treatment of acute pericarditis is 600 mg every 8 hours for 1-2 weeks, followed by a gradual dose reduction.

questions.question: Which condition is characterized by reproducible pain on chest palpation?
questions.explanation: Costochondritis (Tietze Syndrome) is differentiated by localized pain, reproducible on palpation of the costosternal or costochondral joints, without inflammatory or effusion cardiac findings.

questions.question: In a hospitalized patient, what is a key activity restriction?
questions.explanation: For a patient hospitalized with acute pericarditis, complete bed rest is indicated during the acute phase of pain and fever, as well as strict restriction of any physical exertion.

questions.question: What happens if the accumulation of pericardial fluid is rapid or voluminous?
questions.explanation: If the accumulation of pericardial fluid is rapid or very voluminous, the intrapericardial pressure can exceed the diastolic filling pressure of the cardiac chambers, leading to cardiac tamponade.

questions.question: What type of cancer frequently causes metastatic pericarditis?
questions.explanation: Metastases from lung, breast, lymphoma, leukemia, and melanoma carcinomas are common causes of secondary neoplastic pericarditis, with lung cancer being one of the most common.

questions.question: What describes the characteristic pain of acute pericarditis?
questions.explanation: The chest pain of pericarditis is typically pleuritic (worse with deep inspiration, cough) and improves with sitting and leaning forward, positions that decrease pressure on the pericardium.

questions.question: What finding is possible on a chest x-ray with massive pericardial effusion?
questions.explanation: A chest x-ray, although generally normal in pericarditis without significant effusion, may show 'water bottle' cardiomegaly if the effusion is massive (>250-300 mL).

questions.question: What drug interaction should be warned with colchicine?
questions.explanation: Colchicine should be used with caution due to drug interactions with macrolides (erythromycin, clarithromycin), cyclosporine, diltiazem, verapamil, and statins, increasing the risk of toxicity such as myopathy.

questions.question: How is recurrent pericarditis defined?
questions.explanation: Recurrent pericarditis is defined as the reappearance of symptoms after a symptom-free interval of at least 4-6 weeks following a documented initial episode of acute pericarditis.

questions.question: Which region has a high prevalence of tuberculous pericarditis?
questions.explanation: In developing countries, especially in regions with a high prevalence of tuberculosis such as sub-Saharan Africa, tuberculous pericarditis is a significantly more common cause, accounting for up to 70% of cases.

questions.question: What is a key difference between pericarditis and benign early repolarization?
questions.explanation: Although benign early repolarization may show diffuse concave ST elevation, the key to differentiating it from pericarditis is the absence of chest pain and other signs of inflammation such as PR depression.

questions.question: What is the normal amount of serous fluid in the pericardial space?
questions.explanation: The pericardium normally contains a small amount of serous fluid, between 15-50 mL, which allows cardiac movement without friction and its inflammation can lead to pathological effusion.

questions.question: Which laboratory test should be monitored daily if initially elevated?
questions.explanation: C-Reactive Protein (CRP) is a marker of inflammation that should be monitored daily if initially elevated, until normalized, to follow the inflammatory response and clinical evolution.

questions.question: What drug can cause iatrogenic pericarditis?
questions.explanation: Some drugs such as Procainamide, hydralazine, isoniazid, minoxidil, methysergide, phenytoin and penicillins have been associated with pericarditis as an iatrogenic adverse effect.

questions.question: What describes the quality of a pericardial rub?
questions.explanation: Pericardial rub is often described as a 'harsh', 'squeaky' sound, a 'crunch of new leather' or 'snow crunch', due to the rubbing of swollen leaves.

questions.question: What does gadolinium enhancement on CMR indicate in pericarditis?
questions.explanation: Cardiac Magnetic Resonance (CMR) with gadolinium contrast can identify edema and late enhancement in the pericardium, which is indicative of active inflammation (hyperemia) and/or fibrosis.

questions.question: When is urgent pericardiocentesis indicated?
questions.explanation: Urgent pericardiocentesis is the intervention of choice for cardiac tamponade, as it allows pericardial fluid to be drained, relieve cardiac compression, and hemodynamically stabilize the patient.

questions.question: What minor risk criterion may suggest high risk in combination?
questions.explanation: Immunosuppression (due to HIV, transplants, chemotherapy, etc.) is a minor risk criterion, which in combination with others, may suggest high risk and require hospitalization for intensive investigation.

questions.question: What differentiates pleuritis from pericarditis?
questions.explanation: Pleuritis or pneumonia is differentiated by the presence of pulmonary infiltrates or pleural effusion on chest x-ray or CT, as well as by productive cough and crackles or egophony on auscultation.

questions.question: What type of membrane surrounds the heart in pericarditis?
questions.explanation: Acute pericarditis is an inflammatory disease of the pericardium, which is a double-layered membrane composed of the external fibrous pericardium and the internal serous pericardium.

questions.question: What is a key instruction for discharge patient education?
options: Adherence to treatment and warning signs
questions.explanation: Patient education should include the nature of the disease, the importance of adherence to treatment, restriction of physical activity, and recognition of warning signs to seek medical attention.

questions.question: Is there a difference in the incidence of pericarditis between men and women?
questions.explanation: There is a slight preponderance in men compared to women, with an approximate ratio of 1.5:1 to 2:1, in the incidence of acute pericarditis, predominantly affecting young adults.

questions.question: What causes elevated troponins in some cases of pericarditis?
questions.explanation: Elevation of cardiac biomarkers such as troponin indicates concomitant myopericarditis, where pericardial inflammation extends to the underlying myocardium, causing cellular damage.
